This helps frame how investors think about its earnings power and capital needs. At the same time, broader commentary on metals and mining has kept attention on how companies like ArcelorMittal might respond to changes in commodity prices and investment cycles.

Approach 1: ArcelorMittal Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s value to estimate what the entire business could be worth right now.

For ArcelorMittal, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, built on cash flow projections in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$542.0m. Analysts provide estimates for the next few years, and Simply Wall St extrapolates beyond that, with projected free cash flow of US$3.5b in 2030.

Across the ten year projection period, forecast free cash flows range from roughly US$1.5b in 2026 to around US$3.9b by 2035, with each future year discounted back to reflect the time value of money. Adding those discounted cash flows gives an estimated intrinsic value of €65.11 per share.

Compared with the current share price of €51.50, the DCF output suggests ArcelorMittal is trading at about a 20.9% discount, indicating the shares may be undervalued under these assumptions.

Approach 2: ArcelorMittal Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to see how much investors are paying for each unit of earnings. It ties the share price directly to current profits, which many investors watch closely.

In general, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E ratio, while lower growth expectations or higher risk tend to align with a lower, more cautious multiple. What looks “normal” therefore depends on what the market expects from the company’s future earnings and how predictable those earnings appear.

ArcelorMittal currently trades on a P/E of 14.51x, compared with an average of 19.98x for the broader Metals and Mining industry and 26.69x for its peer group. Simply Wall St’s proprietary “Fair Ratio” for ArcelorMittal is 22.44x, which reflects factors such as the company’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can often be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the industry, because it adjusts for the company’s own characteristics rather than assuming it should trade exactly in line with others. On this basis, ArcelorMittal’s current P/E sits below the Fair Ratio, which indicates that the shares screen as undervalued on this metric.

data.title: Insiders Buying Corero Network Security Might Wish They Invested More, Stock Gains 10.0%
data.description:
Corero Network Security plc (LON:CNS) insiders who purchased shares in the last 12 months were richly rewarded last week. The stock climbed by 10.0% resulting in a UK£6.1m addition to the company’s market value. Put another way, the original US$65.2k acquisition is now worth US$72.1k.

While insider transactions are not the most important thing when it comes to long-term investing, logic dictates you should pay some attention to whether insiders are buying or selling shares.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

The Last 12 Months Of Insider Transactions At Corero Network Security

The CEO & Director Carl Herberger made the biggest insider purchase in the last 12 months. That single transaction was for UK£38k worth of shares at a price of UK£0.15 each. So it's clear an insider wanted to buy, even at a higher price than the current share price (being UK£0.13). Their view may have changed since then, but at least it shows they felt optimistic at the time. In our view, the price an insider pays for shares is very important. As a general rule, we feel more positive about a stock if insiders have bought shares at above current prices, because that suggests they viewed the stock as good value, even at a higher price.

Corero Network Security insiders may have bought shares in the last year, but they didn't sell any. Their average price was about UK£0.12. Although they bought at below the recent share price, it is good to see that insiders are willing to invest in the company. You can see the insider transactions (by companies and individuals) over the last year depicted in the chart below. If you click on the chart, you can see all the individual transactions, including the share price, individual, and the date!

Check out our latest analysis for Corero Network Security

Insider Ownership

Looking at the total insider shareholdings in a company can help to inform your view of whether they are well aligned with common shareholders. We usually like to see fairly high levels of insider ownership. Corero Network Security insiders own 41% of the company, currently worth about UK£28m based on the recent share price. I like to see this level of insider ownership, because it increases the chances that management are thinking about the best interests of shareholders.

So What Does This Data Suggest About Corero Network Security Insiders?

The fact that there have been no Corero Network Security insider transactions recently certainly doesn't bother us. But insiders have shown more of an appetite for the stock, over the last year. It would be great to see more insider buying, but overall it seems like Corero Network Security insiders are reasonably well aligned (owning significant chunk of the company's shares) and optimistic for the future. In addition to knowing about insider transactions going on, it's beneficial to identify the risks facing Corero Network Security.
